title: Flash Sale — System Design
description: Design a flash-sale / drop system: sell a small, fixed inventory to a huge, simultaneous crowd. The themes are surviving an extreme traffic spike (orders of magnitude over steady state), admitting users fairly, decrementing limited stock without overselling, and degrading gracefully.

nodes.label: Buyer Client
nodes.description: Floods the system at sale start trying to buy limited stock.

nodes.label: CDN / Static Edge
nodes.description: Serves the static product/landing page so reads don't hit the backend.

nodes.label: Edge / API Gateway
nodes.description: First line of admission control: rate-limit, bot-filter, shed load.

nodes.label: Virtual Waiting Room
nodes.description: Admits a fair, capped rate of users into the purchase path.

nodes.label: Admission Token Service
nodes.description: Issues signed, time-boxed tokens that gate the buy endpoint.

nodes.label: Purchase Service
nodes.description: Validates a token and attempts to reserve one unit of stock.

nodes.label: Inventory Counter
nodes.description: Atomic stock counter (e.g. Redis) decremented per successful reserve.

nodes.label: Order Queue
nodes.description: Buffers confirmed reservations for async order creation.

nodes.label: Order Service
nodes.description: Creates the durable order from a reservation.

nodes.label: Order Store
nodes.description: Authoritative orders and the durable stock ledger.

nodes.label: Payment Service
nodes.description: Charges the buyer within the reservation hold window.

functional: Sell a fixed, limited inventory starting at a precise moment.
Let an admitted user buy (typically) one unit.
Create a durable order and charge the buyer for each successful purchase.
nonFunctional: Survive an extreme spike: demand can be 100-1000x the stock at T0.
Never oversell — at most `stock` units are sold.
Fairness: admitted users get a real chance; no unfair advantage to bots.
Graceful degradation: when overloaded, shed load cleanly (clear 'try later'), not crash.
The vast majority will fail to buy — failure must be cheap and fast.

capacity.label: Concurrent users at T0
capacity.note: All arriving in the same second.

capacity.label: Demand:supply
capacity.note: Most requests must be rejected cheaply.

capacity.label: Steady-state load
capacity.note: The system is overprovisioned only for the spike — or sheds instead.

api.description: Load the (static, CDN-cached) sale page. Does not touch the backend.
Request admission. Joins the virtual waiting room and may receive a signed admission token.
Attempt to buy. Requires a valid admission token; reserves one unit if stock remains.

dataModel.name: inventory_counter (in-memory)
dataModel.note: The hot atomic counter that gates reservations.

dataModel.name: stock_ledger
dataModel.note: Durable source of truth for units allocated; reconciled against the counter.

patterns.name: Static edge offload (CDN)
patterns.what: Serve the sale page and read state from the CDN so the backend only sees buy attempts.
patterns.whenToUse: When read traffic dwarfs the actual transactional work.

patterns.name: Admission control / load shedding
patterns.what: Reject or queue most traffic at the edge so the core only handles a survivable rate.
patterns.whenToUse: Demand far exceeds capacity and most requests can't succeed anyway.

patterns.name: Virtual waiting room
patterns.what: Hold users in a fair queue and admit a capped rate into the purchase path.
patterns.whenToUse: Fairness + protecting the core under a massive simultaneous arrival.

patterns.name: Atomic counter reservation
patterns.what: Decrement a single in-memory stock counter atomically; success means you hold a unit.
patterns.whenToUse: Fast, oversell-proof allocation of limited stock under extreme concurrency.

patterns.name: Async order processing
patterns.what: Confirm the reservation instantly, then create the durable order and charge off the hot path.
patterns.whenToUse: Keep the contended path tiny; do durable/slow work behind a queue.

patterns.name: Reconciliation + reaping
patterns.what: Reconcile the fast counter to the durable ledger and release reservations that don't pay in time.
patterns.whenToUse: Fast-counter designs that must stay consistent with durable truth.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Everyone Hits Buy, Decrement Stock in the DB
description: Start with the obvious design: expose a buy endpoint, let every user call it, and on each call read the stock row from the database, check it's above zero, and decrement it. One transaction per purchase, the DB as the single source of truth. For a normal store this is exactly right.

Under a flash sale it melts. Millions of users arrive in the same second for a few thousand units, so the buy endpoint and the database take the full flood — far past what the DB can serve. Worse, 'read then check then decrement' as separate steps lets concurrent buyers all read the same positive count and oversell. Even a single row with `UPDATE ... WHERE qty > 0` becomes a row-lock hotspot that collapses throughput, and most of the traffic is doomed to fail anyway but still costs a full request.
So the naive path motivates the whole design: offload reads at the edge, admit only a survivable, fair rate, and reserve stock with one atomic counter instead of a contended DB row.
steps.decisionPrompt: 10 million users hit the buy endpoint in the same second for 10,000 units, each doing a read-check-decrement on one stock row. What breaks first?
whyNow: Letting everyone hit the DB makes the two failures concrete — the spike that melts the database and the race that oversells — which is exactly what edge offload, admission control, and the atomic counter remove.

recap.after: Every buyer calls the endpoint and read-check-decrements the stock row in the DB.
recap.newRisk: The spike overwhelms the DB, separate read/check/decrement steps oversell, and a single hot row becomes a lock bottleneck.

traps.trap: Letting the full crowd transact directly against the database.
traps.why: The spike is orders of magnitude over capacity, and naive read-then-decrement oversells while a hot row serializes throughput.
traps.instead: Offload reads to the edge, admit a fair capped rate, and reserve with a single atomic decrement-if-positive counter.

steps.title: 2. Offload Reads to the Edge
description: Most of the flood is people loading the page and refreshing for availability — not actually transacting. Serve the sale page as static assets from a CDN, and expose availability/sold-out as a cacheable, frequently-updated value the page polls.
This keeps the overwhelming majority of traffic off the backend entirely. The backend should only ever see admission and buy attempts.
Push the sale start as a precise time the client honors, so the spike is a known instant you design for rather than a surprise.
steps.decisionPrompt: What fraction of flash-sale traffic is actually trying to transact vs just looking?

concepts.definition: Serving pages and read-only state from CDN cache rather than the origin.
concepts.whyItMatters: Absorbs the bulk of the spike before it reaches any backend.
concepts.example: Sold-out flag cached at the edge with a 1s TTL; the page polls the CDN, not the DB.

whyNow: Cutting read load at the edge is the cheapest, highest-leverage move; everything after only has to handle the residual transactional traffic.

recap.after: Reads absorbed by CDN; backend sees only buy/admission attempts.
recap.newRisk: Even the residual transactional traffic at T0 can swamp the core — need admission control.

traps.trap: Letting the page poll the backend for availability.
traps.why: Millions polling per second is itself a DDoS on your own DB.
traps.instead: Serve availability from the CDN edge with a short TTL.

steps.title: 3. Admission Control at the Edge
description: Demand is 100-1000x stock, so most requests cannot succeed. Reject early and cheaply: rate-limit per user/IP, filter bots, and shed excess load at the edge with a fast, clear 'try later' instead of letting it reach the core.
The goal is to protect the purchase core so it only ever receives a survivable request rate — admitting somewhat more than `stock` (to cover non-completers) but nowhere near the full flood.
Failure here must be the cheap, common case: a shed request should cost almost nothing.
steps.decisionPrompt: If 10M users hit at once for 10K units, what should happen to the other 9.99M requests?

concepts.definition: Deliberately rejecting excess requests to keep the system within capacity.
concepts.whyItMatters: A system that sheds cleanly survives; one that tries to serve everyone collapses.
concepts.example: Edge returns 429/try-later once admitted rate hits the cap.

whyNow: Admission control is the core survival mechanism; stating that most traffic must be rejected cheaply is the key senior insight.

recap.before: All transactional traffic reaches the core.
recap.after: Edge sheds the excess; core sees a survivable rate.
recap.newRisk: Raw shedding is unfair (luck of the millisecond) — need fairness.

steps.title: 4. Fairness: Virtual Waiting Room
description: Pure load-shedding is a lottery on network timing and favors bots with fast connections. A virtual waiting room makes admission fair: users join a queue and are admitted at a controlled rate.

Admission grants a signed, time-boxed token that the buy endpoint requires. The token caps how many users can be in the purchase path at once and ties a buy attempt to an admitted identity (anti-abuse).
Communicate position/wait so users don't hammer retry; the waiting room itself must be cheap (often edge/CDN-backed) since everyone passes through it.

options.name: FIFO virtual waiting room (default)
options.description: Arrivals are held in a durable ordered queue at the edge and admitted in arrival order behind signed admission tokens, metered to match inventory drain.
pros: Admits buyers in arrival order, which feels fair and is easy to communicate ('you are number 42,001 in line').
Decouples the 10M arrivals from the purchase core by holding them behind signed admission tokens.
A position-based queue lets you show progress and meter admission to match inventory drain.
cons: Requires a durable, ordered queue that itself must absorb 10M concurrent entrants.
Strict ordering rewards whoever's network was fastest at the start gun, not a true lottery.
Queue position must be tracked per user, adding state to the admission tier.

options.name: Random lottery admission
options.description: Entrants in a time window are collected into a set, and the waiting room draws admittees at random, sizing each draw to available inventory.
pros: Removes the fastest-network advantage — every entrant in a window has equal odds.
No need to maintain strict global ordering, just a set of entrants per draw.
Naturally smooths the admission rate by sizing each draw to inventory.
cons: Feels arbitrary to users who can't see a position or progress.
Must still collect and dedupe the full entrant set before drawing.
Losers may re-enter, complicating fairness across multiple draws.

options.name: Token-bucket throttle (no ordering)
options.description: The edge admits requests at a fixed rate from a token bucket until inventory is gone and drops the rest, keeping no per-user queue position.
pros: Simplest to operate: admit at a fixed rate (e.g. 500/s) until inventory is gone, drop the rest.
No per-user queue state, so the admission tier stays nearly stateless.
Caps load on the purchase core precisely regardless of arrival burst.
cons: No fairness guarantee — retrying clients with better timing get admitted repeatedly.
Rejected users get a hard 'try again' with no sense of position or chance.
Bursty retries can still hammer the edge even when buckets are empty.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you make admission fair rather than 'whoever's packet arrived first'?

concepts.definition: A queue that admits a capped, fair rate of users into the protected path with a token.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns a stampede into an orderly, throttled, fairer flow and gates the core.
concepts.example: Admit N users/sec with signed tokens; the rest wait with a position.

concepts.definition: A time-boxed, verifiable token required to call the buy endpoint.
concepts.whyItMatters: Caps purchase-path concurrency and blocks un-admitted/bot traffic.
concepts.example: buy requires a valid token; expired/forged tokens are rejected at the edge.

whyNow: Fairness is an explicit requirement and the differentiator from a generic rate limiter; the token also becomes the gate for the buy path.

recap.after: Fair, throttled admission with tokens gating the core.
recap.newRisk: Admitted users now contend on the single scarce resource: stock.

steps.title: 5. Oversell-Proof Reservation
description: Admitted buyers contend for limited stock. The whole correctness requirement reduces to: at most `stock` reservations succeed. Use a single atomic counter (e.g. Redis DECR) — decrement-if-positive returns success only while units remain.
An atomic counter is the contention point but is extremely fast and trivially oversell-proof: the (stock+1)th decrement returns 'sold out'. No locking, no race.
Require the admission token and an idempotency key so a retry doesn't consume two units. Success here is a reservation, not yet a durable order.

options.name: Redis atomic decrement-if-positive (default)
options.description: A single in-memory Redis counter is atomically decremented only while positive, rejecting oversell at memory speed before the slower durable order write.
pros: A single-threaded atomic counter decrement is O(1) and rejects oversell at memory speed.
Handles the 10k-buyers-in-one-millisecond burst without row contention in the database.
Decouples the hot reservation check from the slower durable order write.
cons: The counter is in-memory, so a node crash needs reconciliation against the durable ledger.
A single hot key is a throughput ceiling and must be replicated/failover-ready.
Two sources of truth (counter and ledger) must be reconciled after failures.

options.name: DB row lock / SELECT FOR UPDATE
options.description: Each buy locks the stock-count row in the order database and decrements it within a transaction that also inserts the order, so stock and order commit together.
pros: Single source of truth: the stock count lives in the durable order store, no reconciliation.
Transactional correctness — the decrement and the order insert commit together.
Familiar relational semantics, easy to reason about.
cons: 10k concurrent buyers serialize on one row, collapsing throughput to lock-acquisition rate.
Lock contention causes timeouts and retries that amplify load — exactly the naive failure.
The database becomes the bottleneck the whole design was built to avoid.

options.name: Pre-minted reservation tokens
options.description: Exactly one single-use claim token is minted per unit up front, and holding a token from the pool structurally guarantees a unit, so oversell cannot occur.
pros: Mint exactly 100 single-use claim tokens up front; possessing one guarantees a unit.
Oversell is structurally impossible — there are only as many tokens as units.
Token check can be distributed/sharded, removing the single hot counter.
cons: Unredeemed tokens (abandoned carts) strand inventory until they expire and are reclaimed.
Need a TTL + reclaim process to recirculate tokens, adding machinery.
Fairly distributing the finite token pool reintroduces an admission decision.

steps.decisionPrompt: 10,000 admitted users hit buy at the same millisecond for 100 units. How do you guarantee exactly 100 succeed?

concepts.definition: A single atomic operation that reserves a unit only while the counter is above zero.
concepts.whyItMatters: Oversell-proof under unlimited concurrency with no locks.
concepts.example: Redis DECR returning >= 0 = reserved; < 0 = sold out (with a guard).

whyNow: The atomic counter is the precise mechanism that makes overselling impossible; collapsing the requirement to one atomic op is the senior move.

recap.before: Stock could be oversold under concurrency.
recap.newRisk: Reservations must become durable orders + payment without re-contending on the hot counter.

traps.trap: Read stock, check > 0, then decrement in separate steps.
traps.why: Concurrent buyers all read the same positive value and oversell.

traps.trap: Decrementing a SQL row per buy under the full spike.
traps.why: Row-lock contention on one row collapses throughput.
traps.instead: An in-memory atomic counter as the hot reservation gate; reconcile to durable storage off the hot path.

flows.name: Token-gated atomic reservation
flows.note: An admitted buyer presents a token; the purchase service verifies it, then a single atomic decrement-if-positive either reserves a unit or returns sold-out — oversell-proof and lock-free.

steps.title: 6. Async Order Creation and Payment
description: Keep the contended buy path tiny: a successful counter decrement returns 'reserved' immediately and enqueues the reservation. Order creation, persistence, and payment happen asynchronously off the hot path.
A worker creates the durable order, decrements the durable stock ledger, and charges the buyer within a reservation hold window. The slow/durable work is now decoupled from the spike.
If payment fails or times out, the reservation expires and the unit is released back — so a non-paying winner doesn't permanently consume stock.

options.name: Async order + payment via queue (default)
options.description: The buy request returns the moment the reservation succeeds, then an order service consumes a durable queue to create the order and call the payment gateway off the hot path.
pros: The buy request returns the instant the reservation succeeds, keeping admitted-user latency low.
Order creation and the slow payment call run off a durable queue, absorbing the burst.
A failed charge can retry or release the reservation without blocking the buyer's request.
cons: The buyer is told 'reserved', not 'paid' — the UX must explain the pending state.
Requires an idempotent order pipeline and a path to release stock if payment ultimately fails.
End-to-end completion is eventually consistent, complicating confirmation emails/receipts.

options.name: Synchronous order + charge in the buy request
options.description: The buy request creates the order and calls the payment gateway inline, returning only once the charge is confirmed, with no queue or worker tier.
pros: The buyer gets a definitive 'paid and confirmed' before the response returns — simplest UX.
No pending state, no reconciliation between reservation and payment outcome.
Fewer moving parts: no queue or worker tier to operate.
cons: The slow third-party payment call (hundreds of ms) holds the request open under peak load.
Payment-gateway latency or outages directly fail buys and pin server threads.
Throughput is capped by the slowest dependency in the chain, undermining the whole sale.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why not create the order and charge the card synchronously inside the buy request?
whyNow: Pushing durable/slow work behind a queue keeps the contended path microsecond-cheap; it's why the design survives the spike.

concepts.definition: A time limit for an admitted winner to complete payment before the unit is released.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents non-payers from permanently locking scarce stock.
concepts.example: Reserve -> 10 min to pay -> else expire and release the unit.

recap.before: Heavy work on the contended path.
recap.after: Buy returns instantly; orders/payment processed async with a hold.
recap.newRisk: The fast counter and the durable ledger can drift; expired reservations need releasing.

failureDrills.scenario: A reserved buyer never completes payment.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The reservation expires after the hold window; the unit is released (counter incremented, ledger adjusted) and can resell.
failureDrills.mitigation: Reaper job on reservation TTL + counter/ledger reconciliation.

flows.name: Async order creation + payment
flows.note: A successful reservation is processed off the hot path: a worker creates the durable order, decrements the ledger, and charges the buyer within the hold window; failure releases the unit.

steps.title: 7. Consistency, Reconciliation, and Degradation
description: The in-memory counter is fast but not durable; the order store/ledger is the source of truth. Reconcile them: confirmed orders + live reservations must equal units allocated, and the counter is rebuilt from the ledger after a restart.
Released (expired) reservations increment the counter back. A reaper enforces hold-window TTLs. Periodic reconciliation catches any drift, with the durable ledger authoritative.
Degrade gracefully: if a component is overwhelmed, fall back to shedding (clear 'sold out / try later'), keep the sold-out state correct, and never oversell even under partial failure.
steps.decisionPrompt: The Redis counter node restarts mid-sale. How do you avoid overselling or losing stock?

concepts.definition: Keeping the fast in-memory counter consistent with the durable allocation ledger.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets you use a fast counter as the gate while the durable store stays authoritative.
concepts.example: On restart, remaining = total - confirmed - live_reservations (from the ledger).

whyNow: Closing on reconciliation + graceful degradation shows the fast-path design stays correct under failure — the no-oversell guarantee survives.

recap.after: Counter reconciled to durable ledger; failures shed cleanly; no oversell.
recap.newRisk: Reconciliation lag and reaper timing — bounded by conservative holds and the durable ledger as authority.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Flash Sale
finalDesign.description: The CDN serves the static page and cached availability so the backend only sees transactional traffic. The edge enforces admission control (rate-limit, bot-filter, shed), and a virtual waiting room admits a fair, capped rate of users with signed tokens. Admitted buyers hit the purchase service, which verifies the token and performs a single atomic decrement-if-positive on an in-memory inventory counter — oversell-proof and lock-free. Success returns 'reserved' and enqueues the reservation; a worker creates the durable order, decrements the ledger, and charges within a hold window, releasing the unit on timeout. A reaper + reconciliation keep the fast counter consistent with the authoritative ledger, and the system degrades by shedding rather than overselling.

functional.how: Admission-gated buy path with an atomic stock counter.
Async order creation and payment within a hold.

nonFunctional.how: Virtual waiting room admits a capped, fair rate.
Shed cleanly with 'try later'; keep sold-out correct.
Rejections happen at the edge before the core.

say: Establish demand >> supply (100-1000x) and that most requests must fail cheaply.
Pin never-oversell, fairness, and graceful degradation as the hard goals.
Note the spike is at a known instant you design for.
Admission control + load shedding at the edge.
Virtual waiting room for fairness, gating with tokens.
Oversell-proof reservation via a single atomic counter.
Async order creation + payment with a reservation hold.
Reconciliation, reaping expired holds, and degradation.
Tradeoffs: how many to admit vs stock, counter durability, hold-window length.
Mention anti-bot/abuse and per-user purchase limits as extensions.

expectations: Uses a CDN and an atomic counter to avoid oversell.
May miss fairness and async order handling.
Reasons about counter/ledger reconciliation and restart safety.
Discusses fairness, anti-abuse, and how many to admit vs stock quantitatively.

followUps: How many users do you admit relative to stock, and why?
How do you defend against bots gaming the waiting room?
How would you enforce a per-user purchase limit under this concurrency?
How do you make the in-memory counter highly available without overselling?
How would you handle multiple SKUs / sizes in the same drop?
